posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
There have already some indications that it was a hot year, after Lafleur left the Pomerol appellation citing among other things the heat of the 2025 growing season.I am sure we will see more reports, but I was struck by this comment after the start of white wine harvest at Chateau Carbonnieux.

“The harvest used to begin on September 15, you see, and today is August 19. This global warming phenomenon is now much more reminiscent of Mediterranean climates," Eric Perrin, owner of the Château de Carbonnieux.

posts.name: Justin Patrick
posts.cooked: Curious how ‘25 compares to ‘22 weather wise. Anyone have insights? I’m a buyer of at least a little 2025 regardless for my kiddo’s birth year, but would be interested to see how the years compare nonetheless.

posts.name: Russell Faulkner
posts.cooked:
Rained a bit yesterday. Rained more today. Will rain a bit tomorrow. Will be hot at the end of the week.

One producer we work with had 14% on his Merlot on 25 August.

posts.name: JM San Roman
posts.cooked:
I was in Bordeaux for the whole of last week and the talk over there is that 2025 was as good as 2022.

However, everyone seemed very depressed about the pricing prospects. Most were saying that the 2025 vintage would have to go out en primeur at the same price as 2024s…for a wine which is as good as 2022s. One Chateau owner went even further as to say that prices should be even lower as a “cadeau” (gift) to customers that felt they had been ripped off in 2022 by overpaying relative to what they would be able to buy these wines at today.

Of course there were a couple fo producers who said they would try to increase prices a little relative to last year. But all of them said “negotiants” were being very strong opinionated about the impossibility of raising prices from 2024.

I guess we’ll see.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
I was sent this article by Neal Martin. Pretty dismal reading. Hopefully does not infringe any copyright.

vinous.com

Bordeaux: The Crisis Laid Bare (Jun 2025)

Bordeaux is facing its biggest crisis since the 1930s. After speaking candidly to people in and outside of Bordeaux, I felt a duty to depict the sheer scale of the calamity the Bordeaux wine industry is up against.

posts.name: Jim Petrila
posts.cooked: A good vintage at this past EP’s pricing would be great

posts.name: Stephen Pike
posts.cooked:
It’s interesting the mindset still seems to be that 2022 was appropriately priced and anything below that is a discount, rather than 2022 being overpriced in the circumstances.

Really the problem stems from 2021 which was overpriced for a mediocre vintage and then 2022 stepped up from there. 2021s are now being sold at discounts of 30%+ from release price, if they had started there and that had been the baseline to set 2022, I think things would look a lot more even now.

posts.name: Justin Patrick
posts.cooked:
Thanks Juan, appreciate the insight. Would have even more liked to hear comparisons to '16/19/20 but that didn’t ever seem to be in the cards based on the weather reports over the summer. Pricing relief is good though!

As I said, I’m likely a buyer in some amount as birthyear wines for my son, so love to hear “decent-to-good wine at good prices”! Particularly as I’m likely going to actually do some EP buying for the first time in order to get some larger formats etc.

posts.name: Michael Blasco
posts.cooked:
When back vintages are available for 20% or 30% LESS than their release prices, again, it completely undermines the primeur system, not least any financial return on buying the latest inconsistent vintage.

I mean this is the whole issue right here. Why would I pay MORE right now for a wine that I can’t even drink for a few years?

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
m_blasco:

When back vintages are available for 20% or 30% LESS than their release prices, again, it completely undermines the primeur system, not least any financial return on buying the latest inconsistent vintage.

I mean this is the whole issue right here. Why would I pay MORE right now for a wine that I can’t even drink for a few years?

It was an article that needed to be written. Port producers have been dealing with this for decade. Now that there is so much wine from “good” vintages, it is already happening but on a minor scale.

That being said, 2025 should be seen as an opportunity. It is a chance to reset, and bring the wines back as a viable option for the wine lover’s hard earned cash. Keep pricing the same or lower, and hopefully reboot the category.

posts.name: Jim Petrila
posts.cooked: Agree. My recollection is 2024 ep was priced at or below pretty much all back vintage options, but the question was why do purchase if the back vintages are of higher quality. If 2025 is priced the same as '24 (i.e. lower or equal to back vintages) and is of similar or higher quality then I’ll probably be a buyer.

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked:
What a brilliant article! Thanks for posting it Mark, and if @NealM happens to read this, congratulations!

This week, I received a direct offer to buy a case of 2024 EP from Sociando-Mallet. This is the first time ever I have received a direct offer from any château, since they are normally careful not to compete with négociants, although the price was so high anyway, at 26€, that I doubt anyone will mind. But clearly they must be feeling the pinch.

Nothing in the article comes as any great surprise and as you say Mark, it was about time that someone wrote it. The ostrich mentality, fuelled by some EP cheerleaders ITB, including on WB, has gone on far too long.

As Neal writes, it is of course a question of price. Fine Bordeaux will always find buyers at the right price, which of course means a huge reduction for 2025, irrespective of how “wonderful” it is, but for the numerous châteaux with large bank loans to repay, the pill will be hard to swallow. Anyway, that won’t solve the inventory problem.

I think that the real pressure will be on the négociants themselves. Unlike in the past, there has been very little massive discounting via Foires aux Vins and the like for several years now. As Neal writes, it’s true that tastes have changed, but again, at the right price, the wines would sell.

At one stage, all those gigantic warehouses will be full, or the financial pressure will reach a point where finally, someone loses their nerve, stops the false inventory value and starts selling at discounted prices. Then the dam will really break and we will at last see a market functioning properly, with values of previous vintages tumbling to the new levels - because let’s face it, a 30% drop of 2021 prices hasn’t exactly made them attractive, has it? At 70%, why not.

Bordeaux just has to come to terms with the fact that it’s in a market like any other product.

posts.name: JM San Roman
posts.cooked: Great article that reflects exactly the mood and the way of thinking that I perceived in Bordeaux.

posts.name: Alexander Smith
posts.cooked: I am sure the subject of price comes up every year. I think we need to come back to 2019 prices for consumers to really show interest again, but I am sure others will feel differently, or not.

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked:
Jeff_Leve:

What good is a taster without an opinion? Every critic should bring their own taste and personality to their reviews. Else, at least IMO, what good are they?

I pride myself on being consistent. YMMV.

Yes, I quite agree, as I’ve said before - but that shouldn’t stop you from being able to taste all the wines, irrespective of whether you think you’re going to like the style or not. Because if you’re only going to taste the wines you like, what’s the point? Your consistency means that people know the style of the wines that you like so a bad review, explaining why you don’t like it, is useful for those who follow your work.

Personally, I find totally abhorrent the idea that a château should be able to choose which critic is “allowed” to taste their wine, whichever the wine and whoever the critic.

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked:
JulianD:

And why in the world would I buy 25 when I can get 19/20 (good/great vintages) for 30% cheaper (and more age)

Absolutely. I thought the EP 25 was a reasonable price until I looked at the current prices of the 19 and 20. That good old ostrich seems to enjoy the cool sand around its head alright. Don’t they bother to see how older vintages are doing in the market? Don’t they consider whether or not their clients are going to make a decent deal? Presumably not. Presumably they just think they’re entitled to sell their wine at a certain price. Which they will carry on believing until first the clients, then the négociants stop buying, I suppose.

Anyway, it’s pretty simple: the last time any vintage of Pontet Canet actually increased in value post-EP was 2010. And those were very different times.

I know, I know, I make the same boring remarks every year during the EP jamboree, but nothing seems to change. I wonder how many owners bothered to read Neal Martin’s excellent article last year?

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
Bordeaux has always been reactive. They seem to be in a constant battle to fix the errors of previous vintages. It’s interesting to note what happened when they did something totally correct. The 2019 vintage came out after a slew of great ones, Covid,the threat to economy, and the beginnings of the Chinese moving out of the market. They reduce prices, not just a little, but drastically. And, they sold a ton of wine. Even though the cellars were relatively full, It was an excellent campaign.

Within a couple of years, they were second guessing themselves, complaining that they’d undersold the wines. Third guessing, has proven that it was the right choice, and should be the model for the current release.

So, we have a war threatening oil prices, and a downturn to the economy, consumer confidence a historic low, and interest rates have tripled. When interest rates were low, they could afford mistakes. Income may have been affected, but they still had stocks. Now it’s a problem, and if they don’t have a successful campaign bringing in much needed money, the strain may begin be too much.

Negociants have been financing the chateaux for years, and it’s been pretty much one way traffic. If one negociant fails, I can see it developing into like a Bear Stearns type of situation. So yes, the campaign needs to be cheap. Not just low, but low enough to entice people to buy.

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked:
I agree and disagree. About the current situation, I completely agree. About Bordeaux’s reactivity, not so much. PC’s 2019 EP price was 30% cheaper than 2018 - it certainly looked like a bold move at the time, and I’m guessing it sold well, but sadly history has shown that it wasn’t, in fact, low enough. It’s still hovering at around the same price. Today, to really have a chance of selling, the 2025 would have to be at 70€. Whether or not it would make financial sense for them to sell it at that price (minus taxes and commission) I don’t know, but that’s what the market would need.

As you say, there will certainly be casualties at one point if they don’t listen more carefully to the market.

This morning, I was browsing iDealwine and looked at Lafon-Rochet’s prices. I was surprised to see that the 2020, an excellent wine, is selling for around 30€ buyers commission included. Incredible value for money, but there must be lots more examples like that of other châteaux which I’m unaware of. I’ve no doubt that the LR 25 is at least very good too, but to represent value it would need to be at most 25€, and preferably 20€ - which is what the 2004 cost EP. Ridiculous to imagine that, of course - it’s not even a second wine price. I am sure Jacky Lorenzetti didn’t have that price in mind when he bought LR in 2021, and I doubt it’s a sustainable price for the château.

posts.name: Justin Patrick
posts.cooked:
Julian_Marshall:

Presumably they just think they’re entitled to sell their wine at a certain price. Which they will carry on believing until first the clients, then the négociants stop buying, I suppose.

They’ll continue believing it long after anyone stops buying, that’s what state subsidies are for!

posts.name: Jeff Leve
posts.cooked:
Scott_G_r_u_n_e_r:

I agree to disagree. There would be zero value from his review of dv

I accept that is how you see it. But, I don’t agree. One of my goals is pointing out the style of a wine. It’s not my score that matters as much as the context, which is the note. If I’ve correctly identified the character of the wine, I’ve done a good job.

Consistency in a critic matters. And there are very few people who don’t agree with that about me. Lastly, I have written about numerous wines saying a wine is not my style, adding others might like it more than I did.

Or… Be like Alfret who looks for the wines I love, and avoids them. He seeks out the wines I abhor and from to time to time, he takes a shot.

posts.name: Jeff Hullinger
posts.cooked:
Mark_Golodetz:

There have already some indications that it was a hot year, after Lafleur left the Pomerol appellation citing among other things the heat of the 2025 growing season.I am sure we will see more reports, but I was struck by this comment after the start of white wine harvest at Chateau Carbonnieux.

“The harvest used to begin on September 15, you see, and today is August 19. This global warming phenomenon is now much more reminiscent of Mediterranean climates," Eric Perrin, owner of the Château de Carbonnieux.

This is a fascinating thread, and thanks to Mark_Golodetz for initiating it. Carbonnieux is obviously a very well regarded producer of white Bordeaux, and I look forward to tasting their 2025. With that said, their 2024 I find thin and weak, in sharp contrast to the same vintage examples from Châteaux Couhins and Olivier. (BTW the 2012 white Olivier is magnificent. One of the best, most complicated whites I’ve tasted in the past 5 years.)

I’m really looking forward to returning to Bordeaux later this month. Lots of tasting and evaluation ahead.

Hi William, thank you for posting here. Your posts in this thread made me think about a topic I have been wondering about.

Would it be possible to include information/reasons for not including certain producers in WA reports? To me, the absence of information creates space for unnecessary speculations. Maybe schedules just did not align, maybe producers did not think yours or Yohans scores were high enough, or maybe there was a stylistic or qualitative shift (like microbiological issues you mention), and you are no longer a fan of those wines and don’t want to rate them? Or maybe the reason Is completely different - Tertre Roteboeuf?

My point being, is this not something your subscribers should know? I for one would appreciate the information.

posts.cooked: I liked the 2023 a lot Jeff , not light in style . But it’s silly they do not want you to taste the wine .

posts.name: Jeff Leve
posts.cooked:
Herwig_Janssen:

I liked the 2023 a lot Jeff , not light in style . But it’s silly they do not want you to taste the wine .

Hi Herwig

A fun story for you. I was at lunch with a top property, just me, the owner, and their winemaker. They said point blank to me, “You know, you gave us the lowest score of any critic with our 21!” I felt a bit nervous until they added, “That’s OK, we knew you would, it’s not your style. On the other hand, when you like the wine, you give us your highest scores, so now, we like you more, because we know you’re consistent and honest.”

Over the years I’ve had a few estates not happy with my scores. Some have invited me to taste a vertical together, allowing for a mutual discussion on their wines over the years. I’ve reached to others for a frank discussion on how I see their wines. Those tastings created fair relationships. The best DV would allow was a visit without any wine to taste. I declined. I wonder why?

posts.name: Ⓑⓞⓑⓑⓨ Beachy
posts.cooked:
Arv_R:

I would think that would enrage the selected estates even more

Exactly. I assume publications seeking to collect all the big names have two options… they can publicly shame or humiliate the winemakers and convert a potentially short-term learning experience into a take-it-to-the-grave feud. Or, they can take the high road and let readers use their imaginations and allow the winemaker to either dig their own hole or demonstrate some measure of growth in terms of transparency.

I honestly don’t know what the Lurton side is thinking, though. Wine stores will put up the top score from any reviewer on the placard in a retail store. Those who use many sites to triangulate their buying decisions are surely savvy enough to see a lack of a score with multiple publications after multiple down years as a giant red flag. The winemaker in that scenario appears to be doubling down stylistically on something that might not be popular or is at least divisive. I’m certainly not rushing to purchase wines from an estate that is afraid of what some people might think.

I didn’t really need any explanation from TWA, either. I inferred what (perhaps) happened pretty quickly as I’ve been a fan of the Lurton wines from 2016 through 2020 and always look for TWAs reviews on them. I’ve noticed the decline in score mixed with the helpful notes, and I noticed the absence in scores this year after last year’s “Castaing” of serious doubt on the brand’s 2024s. Now that the scores are completely missing and multiple critics are shadow banned, I can only surmise they are throwing a tantrum, as much as I hate to cast them in that light as I’ve loved their wines in the past.
